stimulus: Born in 1899 to a Berber-speaking family in the Atlas Mountains of Morocco, Idris Aksil is today considered one of the most influential figures in early North African photography. In a paper for an art history class, a student claims that Aksil’s photographs have considerable ethnographic value—in his work, Aksil was able to capture diverse elements of Moroccan society, representing his subjects with both dignity and authenticity.
stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support the student’s claim?

answerOptions.content: Aksil took many commissioned portraits of urban merchants in Fez, but he also produced hundreds of images carefully documenting the peoples, festivals, and crafts of Berber communities of the Atlas.
Aksil’s photographs demonstrate a high level of technical skill, as seen in his careful use of natural backlighting to produce striking silhouettes.
During his lifetime, Aksil was known and celebrated both within and outside his native Morocco, as his work was published in places like France, Egypt, and Algeria.
Some of the peoples and places Aksil photographed had long been popular subjects for Moroccan photographers.

rationale: Choice A is the best answer. The student’s claim is that Aksil’s photographs have ethnographic value because they capture diverse elements of Moroccan society. A finding that Aksil photographed both urban merchants in Fez and the peoples, festivals, and crafts of Atlas Berber communities directly supports the breadth and ethnographic value the student describes. Choice B is incorrect because technical skill in lighting is unrelated to the claim about ethnographic value and diversity of subjects. Choice C is incorrect because international reputation does not show that the photographs depict diverse elements of Moroccan society. Choice D is incorrect because the popularity of his subjects among other photographers does not bear on whether his images are ethnographically valuable.
